content:
# Revelation Chapter 7 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus, pages 282-283 | **Source Image:** REV_007_page_001.jpg, REV_007_page_002.jpg

1. After this I saw four angels standing upon the four corners of the earth, holding the four winds of the earth, so that no wind should blow upon the earth or upon the sea or upon any tree.

2. And I saw another angel ascending from the rising of the sun, having the seal of the living God; and he cried out with a loud voice to the four angels to whom it was given to harm the earth and the sea,

3. saying, "Do not harm the earth nor the sea nor the trees, until we have sealed the servants of our God upon their foreheads."

4. And I heard the number of those who were sealed: one hundred and forty-four thousand, sealed from every tribe of the sons of Israel:

5. from the tribe of Judah, twelve thousand sealed; from the tribe of Reuben, twelve thousand; from the tribe of Gad, twelve thousand;

6. from the tribe of Asher, twelve thousand; from the tribe of Naphtali, twelve thousand; from the tribe of Manasseh, twelve thousand;

7. from the tribe of Simeon, twelve thousand; from the tribe of Levi, twelve thousand; from the tribe of Issachar, twelve thousand;

8. from the tribe of Zebulun, twelve thousand; from the tribe of Joseph, twelve thousand; from the tribe of Benjamin, twelve thousand sealed.

9. After these things I looked, and behold, a great multitude which no one was able to number, from every nation and from all tribes and peoples and tongues, standing before the throne and before the Lamb, clothed in white robes and with palm branches in their hands;

10. and they cry out with a loud voice, saying, "Salvation belongs to our God who sits upon the throne, and to the Lamb."

11. And all the angels stood around the throne and the elders and the four living creatures, and they fell before the throne upon their faces and worshiped God,

12. saying, "Amen; blessing and glory and wisdom and thanksgiving and honor and power and strength be to our God unto the ages of the ages. Amen."

13. And one of the elders answered, saying to me, "These who are clothed in the white robes -- who are they, and from where have they come?"

14. And I said to him, "My lord, you know." And he said to me, "These are those who come out of the great tribulation, and they have washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb.

15. Therefore they are before the throne of God, and they serve him day and night in his temple; and the one seated upon the throne shall spread his tabernacle over them.

16. They shall hunger no more, nor thirst anymore; nor shall the sun strike upon them, nor any scorching heat;

17. for the Lamb who is in the midst of the throne shall shepherd them, and shall lead them to springs of living water; and God shall wipe away every tear from their eyes."

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

The three translators achieved consensus on several important aspects of this chapter:

- **vv.5-8**: All three preserve the identical tribal order found in Sinaiticus: Judah, Reuben, Gad, Asher, Naphtali, Manasseh, Simeon, Levi, Issachar, Zebulun, Joseph, Benjamin. All note the omission of Dan and inclusion of both Manasseh and Joseph.
- **v.9**: All three preserve the four-fold universality: nation, tribes, peoples, tongues.
- **v.14**: All three correctly render the paradoxical image of robes washed white in blood, preserving the theological force without explaining it away.
- **v.17**: All three render the Lamb-as-shepherd image faithfully, preserving the role reversal central to Revelation's theology.

---
## Disagreement Log

**Verse 1** — *"having stood" vs. "standing"*
- EMET: "four angels having stood upon the four corners" (preserving perfect participle ΕΣΤΩΤΑΣ)
- LOGOS: "four angels standing upon the four corners"
- PNEUMA: "four angels standing at the four corners"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "four angels standing upon the four corners" with LOGOS. The perfect participle conveys established position, which "standing" captures in English without the awkward "having stood."

**Verse 1** — *"upon" vs. "at"*
- EMET: "upon the four corners"
- LOGOS: "upon the four corners"
- PNEUMA: "at the four corners"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "upon the four corners" with EMET and LOGOS. ΕΠΙ with the accusative is preserved as "upon."

**Verse 1** — *"that [the] wind should not blow" vs. "so that no wind should blow" vs. "so that no wind would blow"*
- EMET: "that [the] wind should not blow upon the earth nor upon the sea nor upon any tree"
- LOGOS: "so that no wind should blow upon the earth or upon the sea or upon any tree"
- PNEUMA: "so that no wind would blow on the land or the sea or against any tree"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's rendering. "So that" properly introduces the purpose clause. PNEUMA's "land" for ΓΗΣ is inconsistent with the cosmic scope; "earth" is preferred.

**Verse 2** — *"having ascended from [the] rising of [the] sun" vs. "ascending from the rising of the sun" vs. "rising from the east"*
- EMET: "having ascended from [the] rising of [the] sun"
- LOGOS: "ascending from the rising of the sun"
- PNEUMA: "rising from the east"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "ascending from the rising of the sun" with LOGOS. ΑΠΟ ΑΝΑΤΟΛΗΣ ΗΛΙΟΥ is a Hebrew-Greek idiom meaning "from the east"; LOGOS preserves the literal imagery while remaining readable. PNEUMA's simplification loses the solar language.

**Verse 3** — *"the slaves of the God of us" vs. "the servants of our God" vs. "his servants"*
- EMET: "the slaves of the God of us"
- LOGOS: "the servants of our God"
- PNEUMA: "his servants" (with expanded "placed the seal of God on the foreheads of")
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "the servants of our God" with LOGOS. ΔΟΥΛΟΥΣ is rendered "servants" in formal biblical English; PNEUMA's "his servants" omits "our God" and restructures the sealing clause.

**Verse 4** — *"the sons of Israel" vs. "the people of Israel"*
- EMET: "every tribe of [the] sons of Israel"
- LOGOS: "every tribe of the sons of Israel"
- PNEUMA: "every tribe of the people of Israel"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "every tribe of the sons of Israel" with EMET and LOGOS. ΥΙΩΝ ΙΣΡΑΗΛ is literally "sons of Israel," the standard biblical designation.

**Verse 9** — *"a great crowd which to number it no one was able" vs. "a great multitude which no one was able to number" vs. "a vast crowd that no one could count"*
- EMET: "a great crowd which to number it no one was able"
- LOGOS: "a great multitude which no one was able to number"
- PNEUMA: "a vast crowd that no one could count"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "a great multitude which no one was able to number" with LOGOS. ΟΧΛΟΣ ΠΟΛΥΣ is rendered "great multitude" (formal) rather than "great crowd" (EMET) or "vast crowd" (PNEUMA). LOGOS's syntax is most natural in English.

**Verse 9** — *"having stood before" vs. "standing before"*
- EMET: "having stood before the throne"
- LOGOS: "standing before the throne"
- PNEUMA: "they stood before the throne"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "standing before the throne" with LOGOS.

**Verse 10** — *"cry out in a great voice" vs. "cry out with a loud voice" vs. "were shouting with a mighty voice"*
- EMET: "they cry out in a great voice"
- LOGOS: "they cry out with a loud voice"
- PNEUMA: "they were shouting with a mighty voice"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "they cry out with a loud voice" with LOGOS. PNEUMA's "shouting" shifts the register and "mighty" alters ΜΕΓΑΛΗ.

**Verse 10** — *"The salvation [belongs] to the God of us, the [one] sitting upon the throne" vs. "Salvation belongs to our God who sits upon the throne"*
- EMET: "The salvation [belongs] to the God of us, the [one] sitting upon the throne, and to the Lamb"
- LOGOS: "Salvation belongs to our God who sits upon the throne, and to the Lamb"
- PNEUMA: "Salvation belongs to our God, who sits on the throne, and to the Lamb"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's rendering. The definite article before "salvation" in EMET's version is unnecessary in English; the relative clause "who sits" smoothly incorporates the participial phrase.

**Verse 11** — *"had stood" vs. "stood" vs. "stood in a circle"*
- EMET: "all the angels had stood around the throne"
- LOGOS: "all the angels stood around the throne"
- PNEUMA: "all the angels stood in a circle around the throne"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "all the angels stood around the throne" with LOGOS. PNEUMA's "in a circle" is an interpretive addition.

**Verse 12** — *"The blessing and the glory..." vs. "blessing and glory..." vs. "Praise and glory..."*
- EMET: "The blessing and the glory and the wisdom and the thanksgiving and the honor and the power and the strength"
- LOGOS: "blessing and glory and wisdom and thanksgiving and honor and power and might"
- PNEUMA: "Praise and glory and wisdom and thanksgiving and honor and power and strength"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's rendering with one adjustment: "strength" instead of "might" for ΙΣΧΥΣ, to distinguish it from ΔΥΝΑΜΙΣ ("power"). PNEUMA's "Praise" for ΕΥΛΟΓΙΑ is less precise; ΕΥΛΟΓΙΑ means "blessing," not "praise."

**Verse 12** — *"unto the ages of the ages" vs. "forever and ever"*
- EMET: "unto the ages of the ages"
- LOGOS: "unto the ages of the ages"
- PNEUMA: "forever and ever"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "unto the ages of the ages" with EMET and LOGOS, consistent with 5:13.

**Verse 13** — *elder's question phrasing*
- EMET: "These, the [ones] having been clothed in the white robes, who are they and from where did they come?"
- LOGOS: "These who are clothed in the white robes -- who are they, and from where have they come?"
- PNEUMA: "Who are these people dressed in white robes, and where have they come from?"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's rendering. The fronted demonstrative ("These who are clothed") preserves the Greek word order emphasis while remaining readable. PNEUMA's "people dressed in" is too informal.

**Verse 14** — *"I have said" vs. "I said" vs. "I answered"*
- EMET: "I have said to him" (perfect tense ΕΙΡΗΚΑ)
- LOGOS: "I said to him"
- PNEUMA: "I answered"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "I said to him" with LOGOS. English narrative convention favors the simple past.

**Verse 14** — *"My lord" vs. "Sir"*
- EMET: "My lord"
- LOGOS: "My lord"
- PNEUMA: "Sir"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "My lord" with EMET and LOGOS. ΚΥΡΙΕ ΜΟΥ carries deferential weight that "Sir" does not fully convey.

**Verse 14** — *"the great tribulation" vs. "the time of terrible suffering"*
- EMET: "the great tribulation"
- LOGOS: "the great tribulation"
- PNEUMA: "the time of terrible suffering"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "the great tribulation" with EMET and LOGOS. ΤΗΣ ΘΛΙΨΕΩΣ ΤΗΣ ΜΕΓΑΛΗΣ is a specific eschatological designation that should be preserved in its recognized form.

**Verse 14** — *"the [ones] coming out of" vs. "those who come out of" vs. "the ones who have come through"*
- EMET: "the [ones] coming out of"
- LOGOS: "those who come out of"
- PNEUMA: "the ones who have come through"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "those who come out of" with LOGOS. The present participle ΟΙ ΕΡΧΟΜΕΝΟΙ is preserved as an ongoing process. PNEUMA's "come through" changes the preposition ΕΚ (out of).

**Verse 15** — *"On account of this" vs. "Therefore" vs. "That is why"*
- EMET: "On account of this"
- LOGOS: "Therefore"
- PNEUMA: "That is why"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "Therefore" with LOGOS. ΔΙΑ ΤΟΥΤΟ is rendered concisely.

**Verse 15** — *"will tabernacle over them" vs. "shall spread his tent over them" vs. "will shelter them with his presence"*
- EMET: "will tabernacle over them"
- LOGOS: "shall spread his tent over them"
- PNEUMA: "will shelter them with his presence"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts a combined rendering: "shall spread his tabernacle over them." This preserves the Shekinah-tabernacle language (ΣΚΗΝΩΣΕΙ) that EMET rightly identifies, while incorporating LOGOS's "shall spread" for the active covering implied by ΕΠ ΑΥΤΟΥΣ. PNEUMA's "presence" interprets rather than translates.

**Verse 16** — *"will not hunger any longer nor will they thirst any longer" vs. "shall hunger no more, nor thirst anymore" vs. "never again will they be hungry. Never again will they be thirsty"*
- EMET: "They will not hunger any longer nor will they thirst any longer"
- LOGOS: "They shall hunger no more, nor thirst anymore"
- PNEUMA: "Never again will they be hungry. Never again will they be thirsty."
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "They shall hunger no more, nor thirst anymore" with LOGOS. The formal "shall" and concise phrasing are appropriate for this promise. PNEUMA's sentence-splitting adds dramatic emphasis not in the Greek.

**Verse 16** — *"nor shall the sun fall upon them" vs. "nor shall the sun strike upon them" vs. "the sun will not beat down on them"*
- EMET: "nor shall the sun fall upon them"
- LOGOS: "nor shall the sun strike upon them"
- PNEUMA: "the sun will not beat down on them"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "nor shall the sun strike upon them" with LOGOS. ΠΕΣΗ can mean "fall" or "strike"; "strike" better conveys the harmful intent in context.

**Verse 17** — *"living springs of waters" vs. "springs of waters of life" vs. "springs of living water"*
- EMET: "living springs of waters"
- LOGOS: "springs of waters of life"
- PNEUMA: "springs of living water"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "springs of living water." The Sinaiticus reading ΖΩΗΣ ΠΗΓΑΣ ΥΔΑΤΩΝ is rendered with "living" modifying "water" rather than "springs," following the theological pattern established in John 4:10-14 and Revelation 21:6 where ΥΔΩΡ ΖΩΗΣ (water of life/living water) is the key concept.

**Verse 17** — *"the God will wipe away every tear out of the eyes of them" vs. "God shall wipe away every tear from their eyes"*
- EMET: "the God will wipe away every tear out of the eyes of them"
- LOGOS: "God shall wipe away every tear from their eyes"
- PNEUMA: "God will wipe every tear from their eyes"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "God shall wipe away every tear from their eyes" with LOGOS. "Shall" is consistent with the prophetic promises in this passage. PNEUMA omits "away" (ΕΞΑΛΕΙΨΕΙ carries the sense of wiping out/away).

---
*Signed: SOPHIA*
